solution.rawText:
We can do this algebraically.
A translation of the graph of y = f(x) by 2 units in the positive y-direction gives the graph of
y = f(x) + 2
A stretch of factor k parallel to the x-axis gives the graph of y = f( k1 x) = f( xk )
For these to be the same, we need f(x) + 2 = f( k1 x) for all (positive) x.
In our case, f(x) = log10 x, so we require

                                        log10 x + 2 = log10 ( xk )

But log10 ( xk ) = log10 x âˆ’ log10 k, so we require log10 k = âˆ’2, giving k = 10âˆ’2 = 0.01. Hence the
answer is A.

We have done more than required to answer this question: we have actually shown that these
transformations are equivalent. An alternative approach is to assume that the question is correct
and that these transformations are equivalent, and to use a single point to find the value of k.
We could see which point ends up on the x-axis after the translation: it was at (a, âˆ’2), so it
must have been at (0.01, âˆ’2). Therefore the translated graph crosses the x-axis at 0.01, while
the original graph crossed the x-axis at 1. Therefore the stretch needs to be by a factor of 0.01
parallel to the x-axis.
We could have done a similar calculation with any other point.
